# Agricultural Waste: Types, Examples, Causes, Disposal, 2024

![Agricultural waste](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201200%20628'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Agricultural waste](https://www.bindropdumpsters.com/wp-content/uploads/Dumpster-Vs-Compactor-38.png)

Agricultural waste plays a vital role in enhancing sustainability and resource efficiency within the agricultural sector. Farmers use agricultural waste as a natural fertilizer that enriches the soil with essential nutrients and reduces the dependency on chemical fertilizers that degrade soil health over time. By incorporating such waste back into the land, soil structure and moisture retention are improved leading to better crop yields.

The presence of persistent chemical contaminants, such as PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances), in agricultural waste is a growing concern. The contaminants affect the safety and marketability of composts and digestates and lead to stricter regulations on waste management practices. Research is needed to assess the risks associated with the chemical contaminants in soil and crops as well as to develop mitigation strategies.

Types of agricultural waste include crop residues, livestock waste, agro-industrial waste, pesticide and fertilizer runoff, green waste, animal carcasses, plastic waste and food waste. Examples of agricultural waste include wheat straw, rice husks, corn stalks, sugarcane bagasse, cattle manure, poultry droppings, pig slurry, molasses, fruit peels, seed husks, oilseed cakes, excess nitrogen, etc.

Causes of agricultural waste include overproduction, inefficient harvesting and storage, food processing and packaging issues, use of low-quality inputs, livestock waste, pests and disease, water mismanagement, market rejection, post-harvest losses and climate conditions.

This guide explores the different types and causes of agricultural waste, provides examples of common waste materials and outlines effective disposal methods for 2024 to ensure sustainable and eco-friendly practices.

## What is Agricultural Waste?

Agricultural waste refers to organic and inorganic materials produced from farming activities that include crop residues, animal waste, chemical by-products and equipment runoff. Agricultural waste materials require proper management and disposal to minimize environmental impacts and promote sustainable agricultural practices.

Approximately 2 billion tonnes of agricultural waste are generated annually worldwide, with the European Union contributing around 700 million tonnes and the UK producing over 10 million tonnes each year.

In developing countries, the challenge of agricultural waste disposal is compounded by a lack of infrastructure that leads to open burning or improper disposal. Conversely, developed countries are exploring circular economy models and turning waste into biogas organic fertilizers or feedstock for industrial use. Global efforts to manage agricultural waste are critical to achieving sustainability goals and reducing the carbon footprint of the agriculture sector.

## What are the Types of Agricultural Waste?

The types of agricultural waste include crop residues, livestock waste, agro-industrial waste, pesticide and fertilizer runoff, green waste, animal carcasses, plastic waste and food waste. Agricultural waste arises from various farming activities and requires proper management to minimize environmental impact.

Let’s discuss the types of agricultural waste in detail:

### Crop Residues

Crop residues are the remains of plants left in the field after harvest including stems, leaves and roots. Crop residues are produced when crops like wheat, corn and rice are harvested leaving behind non-harvested parts. The residues accumulate in large quantities during peak farming seasons, especially in cereal-producing regions. Crop residues are used as mulch to protect soil and conserve moisture or are processed into animal feed. They are also incorporated into biofuel production or composted to improve soil fertility. Recycling crop residues improves soil organic matter, reduces erosion and increases water retention. They also contribute to sustainable farming by providing a renewable source of nutrients for future crops.

### Livestock Waste

Livestock waste refers to manure and other biological waste produced by farm animals like cattle, pigs and poultry. Livestock waste is generated during the daily care of animals in farms or ranches including their excretion and bedding materials. The quantity of waste is proportional to the size of the livestock operation. Manure is used as an organic fertilizer to enhance soil nutrient levels and promote plant growth. Livestock waste is also processed in biogas plants to generate energy through anaerobic digestion. Livestock waste improves soil health by adding essential nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorus and potassium. Converting manure into energy reduces the carbon footprint and provides renewable energy sources for farms.

### Agro-Industrial Waste

The agro-industrial waste consists of by-products from the processing of agricultural products in industries such as food processing, breweries and sugar mills. The agri-industrial waste is generated during the transformation of raw agricultural commodities into finished goods, producing waste like bagasse, fruit peels and husks. Waste accumulates at various stages of food and beverage manufacturing. Agro-industrial waste is converted into bioenergy, compost or animal feed. The agro-industrial waste is also used in manufacturing biodegradable materials or in value-added products like biofertilizers. Utilizing agro-industrial waste promotes a circular economy by reducing landfill accumulation and enhancing resource efficiency. Processing agro-industrial waste also minimizes the environmental impact of industrial processes by lowering greenhouse gas emissions.

### Pesticide and Fertilizer Runoff

Pesticide and fertilizer runoff refers to the flow of chemicals and nutrients from agricultural fields into nearby water bodies. Runoff occurs when excess pesticides or fertilizers applied to crops are washed away by rainfall or irrigation. Fertilizer runoff is common in areas of intensive farming with high chemical input. While not directly usable, managing runoff through constructed wetlands or buffer strips helps filter pollutants before they reach water bodies. Bioremediation methods also be employed to break down harmful chemicals in the soil. Proper management of runoff reduces water contamination and protects aquatic ecosystems. Managing fertilizer runoff properly also enhances soil quality by preventing the accumulation of toxic chemicals that affect future crop yields.

### Green Waste

Green waste includes biodegradable plant materials like grass clippings, branches and leaves generated during farming and landscaping. Green waste is produced from routine agricultural activities like pruning, mowing and crop harvesting. The green waste is particularly abundant in horticultural and orchard operations. Green waste is composted to produce organic fertilizer or processed into mulch for soil conditioning. Composting green waste reduces landfill usage and promotes soil health by enhancing organic content and microbial activity. Using composting for green waste also supports sustainable energy generation and contributes to renewable energy targets.

### Animal Carcasses

Animal carcasses are the remains of livestock or wildlife that die due to disease, accidents or during culling. Carcasses are produced when animals die unexpectedly or are slaughtered for reasons other than meat production. Farms face the issue of carcasses during outbreaks of animal diseases. Carcasses are rendered to produce animal by-products like bone meal or processed into biofuel. They are also used in composting or anaerobic digestion for energy generation. Proper disposal of carcasses prevents the spread of diseases and helps recycle nutrients into the ecosystem. Rendering reduces waste while generating useful by-products that contribute to waste-to-energy initiatives.

### Plastic Waste

Plastic waste in agriculture consists of discarded plastics like mulch films, seed trays and irrigation pipes used in farming. Plastic waste is generated through the use of plastic materials for crop protection, packaging and water management. Over time, the materials degrade or become obsolete contributing to waste. Recycling plastic waste allows re-usage and helps in producing new agricultural tools or packaging materials. In some cases, plastic waste is incinerated for energy recovery. Recycling plastic reduces environmental pollution and the need for virgin plastic production to conserve natural resources. Recycling plastic waste also helps mitigate the negative impact of plastic on soil and water quality and improves sustainability.

### Food Waste

Food waste is edible material discarded at various stages of the food supply chain from farms to consumers. [Food waste](https://www.bindropdumpsters.com/waste-management/food/) is produced due to factors like overproduction, improper storage or handling during harvest and processing. Large quantities of waste accumulate during post-harvest or transport operations. Food waste is composted to produce organic fertilizers or used in anaerobic digesters to create bioenergy. Surplus food is also redirected for animal feed or food recovery programs. Reducing food waste cuts greenhouse gas emissions from landfills and contributes to global food security. Repurposing food waste for energy and soil amendment promotes sustainable farming practices.

## What are the Examples of Agricultural Waste?

Examples of agricultural waste include wheat straw, rice husks, corn stalks, sugarcane bagasse, manure, poultry droppings, pig slurry, molasses, seed husks, excess nitrogen, phosphorus runoff, pesticide residues, swine carcasses, cattle carcasses, plastic mulch, pesticide containers, spoiled vegetables and rejected fruits.

Here are the details of the examples of agricultural waste:

### Wheat Straw

Wheat straw is the dry stalk left after harvesting wheat grain. The wheat straw is a by-product of wheat farming that is left in fields after harvest. Wheat straw is commonly used as animal bedding, mulch for soil moisture retention or in compost production. Wheat straw is also processed into biofuel or paper pulp. Utilizing wheat straw reduces waste disposal issues and improves soil fertility when used as mulch or compost. Straw as animal bedding enhances livestock welfare by providing insulation. Using wheat straw for multiple purposes contributes to the circular economy by serving as an alternative to non-renewable resources in energy and industry.

### Rice Husks

Rice husks are the protective outer covering of rice grains removed during milling. Rice husks are generated during the rice milling process and accumulate in large quantities in rice-producing regions. They are used in energy production as biomass fuel in building materials like insulation or converted into biochar for soil improvement. Rice husks are also used in animal bedding and compost. Their use as biofuel helps in reducing reliance on fossil fuels. Biochar made from rice husks enhances soil structure and water retention. Repurposing rice husks in construction reduces waste and promotes eco-friendly building materials.

### Corn Stalks

Corn stalks are the stems and leaves left after corn cobs are harvested. Corn stalks are left in the field after harvest and contribute to agricultural waste. They used as livestock feed, mulch or processed into ethanol for biofuel production. Corn stalks are also utilized in biogas plants or converted into biodegradable packaging materials. Using corn stalks as feed helps reduce feed costs for farmers. As mulch, they prevent soil erosion and maintain soil moisture. Processing them into biofuels or packaging promotes sustainability by reducing dependence on non-renewable resources.

### Sugarcane Bagasse

Sugarcane bagasse is the fibrous residue left after extracting juice from sugarcane. Produced during sugar extraction, bagasse is abundant in sugarcane-processing regions. Sugarcane bagasse is commonly used as fuel in boilers to generate electricity and steam in sugar mills or processed into paper, bioethanol or compost. Bagasse helps sugar mills become self-sufficient in energy to reduce fossil fuel usage. As a raw material for paper, sugarcane bagasse reduces reliance on wood-based resources and promotes forest conservation. Bagasse bioethanol serves as an alternative to gasoline contributing to renewable energy goals.

### Cattle Manure

Cattle manure is the solid waste excreted by cattle rich in nutrients like nitrogen and phosphorus. Manure is produced on livestock farms and is an essential organic fertilizer. Cattle manure is applied to fields to improve soil fertility and structure or processed in anaerobic digesters to produce biogas. Manure is also used in composting or as bedding for livestock. The use of cattle manure as fertilizer reduces the need for synthetic fertilizers promoting sustainable farming. Biogas from manure provides renewable energy cutting greenhouse gas emissions. Manure-based compost improves soil health and biodiversity.

### Poultry Droppings

Poultry droppings are the solid waste from chickens and other birds rich in nitrogen. Poultry farms generate droppings in large quantities which are used as a potent organic fertilizer. They are either applied directly to fields or composted to reduce pathogens and odors. Poultry droppings are also used in anaerobic digesters to produce biogas. As fertilizer, they enhance crop yields by supplying essential nutrients. Composting poultry manure improves soil organic matter promoting long-term soil health. Biogas from droppings offers renewable energy solutions and helps reduce the farm’s environmental footprint.

### Pig Slurry

Pig slurry is a mixture of pig manure and urine that is used as liquid fertilizer. Pig slurry is produced in pig farming operations and stored in lagoons or tanks. The pig slurry is applied to fields as a nutrient-rich liquid fertilizer or treated in anaerobic digesters for biogas production. Pig slurry enhances soil nutrient levels, particularly nitrogen and potassium to improve crop growth. When processed into biogas pig slurry provides renewable energy for farms. Proper slurry management reduces greenhouse gas emissions and water pollution promoting sustainable farming.

### Molasses

Molasses is a thick and syrupy by-product of sugar refining. Generated during sugar processing, molasses is used in animal feed, fermentation for ethanol production or as a soil amendment in agriculture. Molasses serves as a carbohydrate-rich supplement in livestock diets promoting weight gain. In ethanol production, molasses is a key raw material for biofuel. Molasses improves soil microbial activity when applied to fields to enhance soil health. The use of molasses in biofuel reduces dependence on fossil fuels to support renewable energy efforts.

### Fruit Peels

Fruit peels are the outer skins of fruits discarded during processing or consumption. Fruit processing industries produce large amounts of peels which are composted, used in animal feed or converted into bioactive compounds. Peels from citrus, apples and bananas are rich in nutrients and fibers. They improve soil organic content when composted and used in cosmetics or dietary supplements. The extraction of pectin from peels provides valuable additives for the food industry. Repurposing peels reduces organic waste and promotes resource efficiency

### Seed Husks

Seed husks are the outer coverings of seeds removed during processing. Seed husks are generated during the de-husking of crops like rice, sunflower and soybeans. They are used as biomass fuel, compost or animal bedding. Seed husks add organic matter to soil when composted to enhance soil structure and fertility. As biomass, they serve as a renewable energy source for heating or electricity generation. Recycling husks reduces waste and contributes to sustainable energy production.

### Oilseed Cakes

Oilseed cakes are the solid residues left after extracting oil from seeds like soybeans or sunflowers. The oilseed cakes are by-products are produced in the oil extraction process and are rich in protein and fiber. Oilseed cakes are commonly used in animal feed, especially for livestock and poultry. They enhance feed quality to provide essential nutrients for animal growth. In some cases, oilseed cakes are used as organic fertilizers. Their use reduces reliance on synthetic feed additives and promotes eco-friendly farming practices.

### Excess Nitrogen

Excess nitrogen refers to the surplus nitrogen left in the soil after the application of fertilizers. Nitrogen fertilizers are often over-applied to crops leading to runoff into water bodies or accumulation in the soil. Excess nitrogen is harmful to ecosystems contributing to water pollution and eutrophication. Managing nitrogen levels through precision agriculture reduces the environmental impact. Proper nitrogen management enhances crop uptake efficiency improving yields while minimizing waste. Reducing nitrogen runoff protects water quality and supports sustainable agriculture.

### Phosphorus Runoff

Phosphorus runoff occurs when excess phosphorus from fertilizers washes into water bodies. Over-application of phosphorus fertilizers leads to the accumulation in soil and eventual runoff during rain. The runoff contributes to water pollution and algal blooms affecting aquatic ecosystems. Managing phosphorus levels through targeted application reduces environmental risks. Proper phosphorus management improves crop nutrient uptake to enhance growth while minimizing waste. Reducing runoff protects water resources and promotes sustainable farming practices.

### Pesticide Residues

Pesticide residues are the remnants of chemical pesticides left on crops or in the soil after application. Pesticides applied to crops leave behind traces that persist in the environment affecting soil and water quality. Residue management involves using bioremediation techniques to break down harmful chemicals. Reducing pesticide use through integrated pest management minimizes residue levels and environmental risks. Safer residue management protects ecosystems and reduces health risks for humans and animals. Sustainable practices promote soil health and biodiversity.

### Pruned Branches

Pruned branches are the cuttings removed during the maintenance of trees and shrubs in agricultural or horticultural activities. Pruning is done to maintain plant health, shape or yield producing large quantities of branches. The branches are chipped into mulch, composted or used as biomass fuel. Mulching with pruned branches improves soil moisture retention and prevents weed growth. Composting returns nutrients to the soil to enhance fertility. Using pruned branches for biomass energy reduces reliance on non-renewable resources.

### Weeds

Weeds are unwanted plants that grow in cultivated fields competing with crops for resources. Weeds are removed during farming to protect crop yields creating organic waste. They are composted or used in mulching to suppress further weed growth. Composting weeds returns organic matter to the soil to enhance fertility. Weed biomass is also used in biogas production. Proper weed management promotes healthier crop growth and reduces the need for chemical herbicides.

### Leaves

Leaves are plant foliage shed naturally or during harvesting creating organic waste. Leaf waste is common in horticulture and farming accumulating during seasons like autumn. Leaves are composted to produce nutrient-rich humus or used as mulch to protect soil. Composting leaves return organic matter to the soil to enhance fertility and structure. Mulching with leaves prevents erosion and helps

### Grass Clippings

Grass clippings are the cuttings left on the ground after mowing grass. Grass clippings are generated during routine lawn maintenance or when mowing pastures. They are left on lawns to decompose naturally, composted or used as mulch in gardens. When left on the lawn they provide nutrients as they break down to reduce the need for additional fertilizers. Using clippings as mulch conserves soil moisture and suppresses weed growth. Composting grass clippings enriches soil, improves soil structure and encourages beneficial microorganisms.

### Dead Poultry

Dead poultry refers to chickens or other birds that die due to disease, accidents or culling. Dead poultry accumulates on farms that require careful disposal to prevent disease spread. They are rendered into animal feed, composted or disposed of in specialized facilities to ensure safe management. Rendering provides protein for livestock feed minimizing waste and promoting efficient resource use. Composting dead poultry returns nutrients to the soil to enhance soil fertility.

### Swine Carcasses

Swine carcasses are the remains of pigs that die due to various causes including disease or accidents. The carcasses must be properly disposed of to prevent disease outbreaks and environmental contamination. They are rendered into various animal by-products, composted or incinerated to safely manage waste. Rendering converts swine carcasses into valuable protein sources for livestock feed to reduce waste. Composting enriches soil health and recycles nutrients benefiting agricultural land.

### Cattle Carcasses

Cattle carcasses are the remains of cattle that die from various causes such as disease or accidents. Proper disposal of cattle carcasses is crucial to prevent disease transmission and protect environmental health. They are rendered into animal by-products, composted or incinerated. Rendering provides a source of protein for livestock feed while minimizing waste. Composting carcasses returns nutrients to the soil to support agricultural practices and enhance soil fertility.

### Plastic Mulch

Plastic mulch is a synthetic covering used in agriculture to suppress weeds and retain soil moisture. Widely used in vegetable production, plastic mulch is wasted at the end of the growing season. Plastic munch is recycled if properly collected and processed to reduce environmental pollution. Recycling plastic mulch conserves resources and supports sustainable agricultural practices. Proper disposal reduces plastic waste and encourages responsible farming methods.

### Irrigation Pipes

Irrigation pipes are tubes used to transport water to crops during irrigation.  
Over time, the pipes become damaged or outdated leading to waste. They are recycled to create new pipes or products to reduce plastic waste and support sustainability. Proper recycling minimizes the environmental impact of irrigation systems and conserves resources. Reusing materials in new applications promotes a circular economy in agriculture.

### Pesticide Containers

Pesticide containers are packaging used for storing and transporting pesticides. Once pesticides are used, the containers come to waste and pose environmental hazards if not disposed of properly. Proper recycling and disposal methods such as triple rinsing, mitigate risks and allow containers to be repurposed. Recycling reduces the need for new materials and minimizes the environmental impact of agricultural practices.

### Spoiled Vegetables

Spoiled vegetables have deteriorated in quality and are no longer fit for consumption. The spoiled vegetables result from overproduction, inadequate storage or transportation issues. They are composted to enrich the soil or processed into animal feed to reduce waste. Composting returns valuable nutrients to the soil and improves soil health. Repurposing spoiled vegetables for animal feed minimizes losses and supports sustainable agriculture. Proper management of spoiled vegetables contributes to food security and environmental sustainability.

### Rejected Fruits

Rejected fruits do not meet market standards due to size, shape or damage. The rejected fruits are discarded during sorting and grading in the supply chain, creating waste. They are processed into juice, jams or composted to reduce waste and generate value-added products. Utilizing rejected fruits in food processing helps minimize economic losses for farmers. Composting rejected fruits contributes organic matter to the soil to enhance soil fertility. Proper management of rejected fruits supports sustainability in the agricultural sector and reduces food waste.

### Post-Harvest Losses

Post-harvest losses refer to the decrease in quantity or quality of agricultural products after harvest and before consumption. The post-harvest losses occur due to inadequate storage, handling or transportation practices. Improving post-harvest practices such as better storage and efficient transport significantly reduces waste and increases food availability. Effective management of post-harvest losses enhances food security and supports sustainable agricultural practices. Reducing losses also improves farmers’ incomes and decreases the environmental impact associated with waste disposal. Addressing post-harvest losses is crucial for creating a more sustainable food system.

## What are the Causes of Agricultural Waste?

The causes of agricultural waste include overproduction, inefficient harvesting and storage, food processing and packaging issues, use of low-quality inputs, livestock waste, pests and disease, water mismanagement, market rejection, post-harvest losses and climate conditions that lead to significant resource losses.

The following are the details of the causes of agricultural waste:

### Overproduction

Overproduction occurs when farmers grow more crops than the market demands. The excess is discarded due to a lack of storage, buyers or transport infrastructure. Economic incentives, subsidies or inaccurate market forecasts drive the surplus. In some cases, farmers overproduce to ensure they meet demand even if part of the crop fails. However, the surplus that isn’t sold or used spoils. Overproducing leads to wasted resources such as water, fertilizers and energy. The practice also depletes soil health in the long run by reducing land productivity.

### Inefficient Harvesting and Storage

Inadequate harvesting methods damage crops or leave a portion of the produce behind which leads to waste. Similarly, improper storage techniques fail to protect crops from pests, moisture and decay. Harvesting too early or late also degrades the quality of produce. In developing regions, lack of access to modern storage facilities exacerbates the problem. Inappropriate storage temperatures or humidity levels cause significant spoilage. Some crops require specialized storage and failure to meet the needs increases losses. Poor handling during transport to storage also contributes to agricultural waste.

### Food Processing and Packaging

A large amount of waste occurs during food processing and packaging when products are trimmed, sorted or discarded if they don’t meet quality standards. Edible parts of fruits, vegetables and meats are wasted in the packaging phase. Inefficient machinery or processes further escalate waste. Packaging materials also lead to environmental waste when not properly managed. The aesthetic preferences of consumers also lead to the rejection of food with minor imperfections. Sometimes, food is processed in ways that shorten the product’s shelf life. The inefficiencies in the supply chain also prevent a significant portion of agricultural produce from reaching consumers.

### Use of Low-Quality Inputs

The use of low-quality seeds, fertilizers and pesticides results l in poor crop yields that leads to higher waste. Substandard inputs also weaken plants and make them more vulnerable to pests and diseases. The low-quality inputs also reduce the nutritional quality of crops to decrease their market value. In some cases, inferior fertilizers or pesticides harm the soil to reduce soil’s long-term productivity. Using outdated or counterfeit products further exacerbates the problem. Low-quality irrigation practices may also prevent crops from growing optimally. As a result, farmers may discard crops that fail to meet quality standards and contribute to agricultural waste.

### Livestock Waste

Livestock waste includes not only manure but also unused animal parts after slaughter. Overproduction of animal products leads to meat spoilage if storage facilities are inadequate. Improper feed management or overfeeding livestock also leads to waste. Slaughtering processes often discard by-products like bones, skin or fat even if they could be repurposed. Manure is mishandled and contributes to environmental pollution rather than being used as fertilizer. Poor animal health or disease outbreaks lead to entire herds being culled and waste valuable resources. Inefficiencies in the dairy or poultry industry also result in significant waste of milk and eggs.

### Pests and Disease

Crops and livestock are vulnerable to various pests and diseases that reduce yields. When infestations occur entire fields are rendered unusable or products rejected. Insufficient pest control or outdated management practices further e

# Agricultural Waste Management

Between crop residues, animal byproducts, pesticides, herbicides, packaging and more, the agriculture industry produces a diverse set of waste streams that pose unique challenges. To manage these materials effectively and sustainably, you’ll need solutions of an equal or greater breadth.

## Comprehensive and complete agricultural waste solutions

We offer a wide scope of sustainable, end-to-end solutions that can handle waste streams of any makeup or magnitude. No matter the challenge at hand, Reworld™ is able to reduce the impact of your agricultural waste and help you discover opportunities to safely reuse and recycle your resources or transform them into renewable fuel or energy.

## Agricultural waste management solutions we offer

### ReDrop wastewater treatment

Chemical and physical treatment that separates water from contaminants for reuse, recycling, energy recovery, or reintroduction into nature.

### Environmental remediation

Site evaluations and waste profiling, emergency remediation and pump-outs, wastewater and hazmat hauling, and a number of other preventative and responsive maintenance services.

### Hazardous waste management

Meticulous, end-to-end services that manage the treatment, handling, and final disposal of lab packs, corrosives, flammables, pesticides, and a number of other toxic materials.

### ReKiln Fuels Alternative Engineered Fuel

Transforms unsalvageable waste materials into tailored, low-carbon fuels ideal for cement and lime kilns, powerplants, paper mills, and more.

## Why partner with Reworld for agricultural waste treatment and disposal?

ReworldTM offers comprehensive and sustainable agricultural waste treatment and disposal solutions, addressing diverse waste streams through animal bed recycling and production, alternative fuel creation, energy recovery, and other innovative technologies. Partnering with ReworldTM helps businesses reduce environmental impact, ensure regulatory compliance, and potentially recover value from otherwise “unsalvageable” waste, contributing to efficient and responsible waste management, as well as a circular economy.

## Agricultural Waste Management FAQ

Agricultural waste includes organic and inorganic materials generated from farming and food production. Common examples include crop residues like stalks, husks, and leaves, animal manure, spoiled or excess produce, wastewater from irrigation or processing, and chemical waste such as pesticides, herbicides, and fertilizer runoff. Packaging materials, plastics, and machinery byproducts also contribute to agricultural waste.

Improperly managed agricultural waste can lead to soil degradation, water pollution, and air contamination, negatively impacting ecosystems and public health. Businesses may face regulatory fines, operational inefficiencies, and reputational risks due to poor waste management practices. Additionally, excess organic waste can produce methane, a potent greenhouse gas, contributing to climate change. However, sustainable waste solutions can turn waste into valuable resources, reducing environmental harm and improving operational efficiency.

Sustainable disposal methods include composting organic waste into nutrient-rich soil amendments, converting waste into bioenergy through anaerobic digestion, and repurposing byproducts for animal feed or industrial applications. Water treatment technologies help manage wastewater, while recycling programs ensure responsible handling of plastics, packaging, and chemical containers, repurposing them or turning them into new products, like animal bedding. For post-recycled materials that are deemed “unsalvageable” and are landfill-bound, alternative engineered fuel solutions and waste-to-energy solutions can process them into renewable, low-carbon fuel or carbon-negative energy. These approaches reduce environmental impact while supporting circular economy principles.

Effective agricultural waste management improves environmental sustainability, regulatory compliance, and resource efficiency. Businesses can reduce greenhouse gas emissions, protect water sources, and enhance soil health through responsible waste handling. Recycling and repurposing waste materials can lower operational costs, create new revenue streams, and contribute to circular economy initiatives. Additionally, sustainable waste practices improve brand reputation and consumer trust in environmentally responsible agriculture.

A strong agricultural waste services partner should have expertise in regulatory compliance, sustainable disposal methods, and waste reduction strategies. They should offer customized solutions, including composting, recycling, energy or fuel conversion, and wastewater treatment. Transparency, reliable service, and a commitment to innovation are key qualities. The ideal partner should also help businesses optimize waste management practices, reduce costs, and align with sustainability goals to enhance long-term operational success.

# Agricultural wastes

Agricultural wastes are organic by-products generated during the agricultural production process, encompassing residues from both crop and animal farming. They include materials such as leaves, straw, husks, and manures, which can be categorized into primary, secondary, and tertiary wastes based on their occurrence along the agricultural value chain. Despite their potential as a renewable energy resource—estimated to equate to about 50 billion tons of oil—the energy potential of agricultural residues remains largely untapped globally. These residues can be converted into various energy forms using technologies such as direct combustion, biogas generation, and fermentation, depending on their specific properties.

The use of agricultural residues as energy sources presents a viable alternative to fossil fuels, particularly as global energy prices rise. However, competition for these residues exists, as they are also valuable for maintaining soil fertility and as animal fodder, especially in developing regions. Environmental concerns arise from the improper disposal and burning of these wastes, leading to greenhouse gas emissions that contribute to climate change. Innovations in valorization techniques aim to recover valuable bioactive compounds from agricultural wastes, enhancing sustainable management practices. Overall, the increasing recognition of agricultural residues as a significant energy source underscores the need for strategic approaches to their utilization while considering environmental impacts and competing uses.

## Subject Terms

# Agricultural wastes

**Summary:**  Agricultural wastes are by-products of the agricultural system that can be converted into various forms of energy using different conversion technologies. Most of the worldwide energy potential from agricultural residues is currently unexploited.

Agricultural wastes are defined as all types of organic substrates, which arise during the process of agricultural production. These organic wastes can come from crop farming, in the form of leaves, straw, husks, [shells](/research-starters/zoology/shells), and hulls, as well as from animal farming in the form of manures, slurries, and animal bedding such as poultry litter. In crop farming, different crops produce different types of agricultural crop wastes. The nature and properties of agricultural crop wastes depend primarily on the plant species, growing conditions, and the processing required to obtain the agricultural product. The amount and composition of animal manure depend on the farming methods (intensive and extensive husbandry), type of animal, and amount and type of feeding.

## Agricultural Waste Resources

Depending on where along the agricultural value chain the agricultural wastes occur—production, provision, usage, or disposal—the agricultural waste can be subdivided in three main categories: primary, secondary, and tertiary wastes. Primary wastes consist of residues remaining in fields after harvest and allocation. They include straw, stalks, cobs, and leaves. Secondary wastes, also known as processing wastes, are residues occurring during processing of the product, such as slaughterhouse wastes, milling residues, fruit peels, husks, bagasse, and filtration and extraction residues. Tertiary wastes are residues remaining after the usage of the product, such as organic municipal residues, postconsumer residues, and landfill gases.

These different types of wastes from agriculture production are a valuable [renewable energy](/research-starters/power-and-energy/renewable-energy) resource. Because they have a material and energy value, the term *agricultural residue* is frequently used instead of *agricultural waste*. The [United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP)](/research-starters/environmental-sciences/united-nations-environment-programme-unep) estimates that the worldwide energy potential of all agricultural residues combined is equivalent to about 50 billion tons of oil. Although this energy can be used to replace unsustainable fuel sources and reduce the dependence on [fossil fuels](/research-starters/environmental-sciences/fossil-fuels), it is largely underutilized in most countries.

Using these residues as energy feedstock entails far fewer risks of land use and resource [competition](/research-starters/environmental-sciences/competition) compared to energy pathways that use food and a energy crops to generate energy. Moreover, it is also one of the lowest-cost bioenergy feedstocks. Secondary residues are particularly suitable for commercial energy generation, because they are usually available in large quantities at the processing sites without additional transportation and handling costs.

When considering agricultural residues as commercial energy source, the following aspects need to be thought about in order to secure a steady supply stream: seasonality of crop production, availability of residues, harvesting method, livestock-keeping systems, transport distance and cost, competing uses, and possible effects of climate change. To use animal manure and bedding as energy substrates, it is further necessary to provide sufficient streams of preferably wet dung. In light of this, the main potential for commercial utilization of animal manure and bedding is allocated on large- or medium-scale cattle, pig, and poultry farms, where animals are generally raised indoors, so that the substrates can be easily collected. Most small-scale farmers, especially in developing countries, keep their livestock free-range, which makes the utilization of animal manure as feedstock a challenging task.

The overall importance of agricultural residues as an energy source will most likely increase in the coming years, because with rising energy prices economical and environmentally feasible alternatives need to be established. Agricultural residues represent a large and available potential source of energy that is ready to be used more extensively.

## Competing Uses of Agricultural Residues

Despite their energy potential, agricultural residues—both animal manure and crop residues—have competing uses, which need to be considered. For instance, agricultural residues are often used as fertilizer to maintain soil carbon content and fertility, because they contain important nutrients. They also provide protection against erosion and can contribute to soil [biodiversity](/research-starters/science/biodiversity). Environmentally sustainable biomass operations need to consider to what extent residues can be removed and what quantities have to be put back on the field in order to preserve the nutrient cycle. Commonly it is recommended to leave, on average, 30 percent of the agricultural crop residue on the fields. Besides their use as fertilizer, crop residues in developing countries are often used as animal fodder. Although agricultural residues are a poor fuel source for direct combustion, they are widely used for cooking and heating in areas with scarce wood fuel, causing health and environmental problems.

Elsewhere, agricultural residues have no use and are improperly disposed or burned on the field or farm, causing environmental problems such as air, water, and soil pollution. Agricultural residues account for a large percentage of biomass burned annually worldwide. Particularly critical are methane, [carbon dioxide](/research-starters/science/carbon-dioxide), and other [greenhouse gas](/research-starters/environmental-sciences/greenhouse-gas) emissions that are released during the decomposition process and open burning of agricultural residues. These emissions are known to contribute to atmospheric warming and climate change.

## Conversion Technologies

A variety of conversion technologies for energy generation with agricultural residues exist, for use in both large and small plants. Further technologies are in development and testing stages. Based on the nature of the transformation process, the different conversion technologies can be divided into three main categories: physical, thermal, and biological methods. The final products can be electricity, heat, and liquid fuel. Electricity and heat can be generated from agricultural residues through direct combustion, combined heat and power technology, or the generation of biogas. Liquid fuels from residues can be produced using fermentation (ethanol) or by pressing or extraction ([biodiesel](/research-starters/environmental-sciences/biodiesel)) processes. The physical and chemical characteristics of agricultural residues vary widely.

Their specific properties determine their quality as [energy resources](/research-starters/environmental-sciences/energy-resources-and-global-warming) and the suitability for the different conversion technologies. Substrates with high moisture content (greater than 60 percent), such as fruit peels and animal manure, are most suitable for biogas production, whereas their moisture content makes them energetically inefficient to use for combustion or **gasification**. For these processes, a relatively low moisture content (<10–15 percent) is required. Moisture contents can be reduced before or as part of the combustion or gasification process, but in both cases the overall efficiency and final energy output are reduced.

During the 2020s, world governments and the scientific community realized that modern agricultural processes created dangerous levels of agricultural waste. To utilize this waste safely, some researchers began experimenting with valorization techniques. These techniques involved using environmentally friendly physical, chemical, and biological processes to recover bioactive compounds.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Crop residues include stalks, leaves, and husks left after harvesting, providing organic matter for soil enrichment, reducing erosion, and serving as livestock feed or biofuel.
Livestock waste encompasses manure and bedding materials, rich in nutrients, that can be composted or processed into biogas, contributing to soil fertility and renewable energy.
Agro-industrial waste consists of by-products from processing agricultural goods, such as fruit peels and milling residues, which can be repurposed into bioenergy or organic fertilizers.
Food waste arises from overproduction and improper storage, leading to composting opportunities or conversion into animal feed, reducing landfill contributions and enhancing food security.

Improper management of agricultural waste leads to soil degradation, water pollution, and greenhouse gas emissions, necessitating sustainable practices to mitigate ecological harm and enhance resource efficiency.
